cards.question: Why is finesse emphasized over force in airway management?
cards.answer: Forceful, rushed attempts cause trauma and hypoxia; a calm, prepared, deliberate approach with good team communication produces better outcomes.

cards.question: How do you measure a suction catheter before insertion?
cards.answer: From the corner of the mouth to the earlobe (or angle of the jaw); insert without suction and apply suction only on withdrawal.

cards.question: How long should a single suction attempt last in an adult?
cards.answer: No more than about 15 seconds (less in children), because suction also removes oxygen; reoxygenate between attempts.

cards.question: How is an oropharyngeal airway (OPA) sized?
cards.answer: From the corner of the mouth to the earlobe or angle of the jaw.

cards.question: How is a nasopharyngeal airway (NPA) sized?
cards.answer: From the nostril to the earlobe; diameter roughly matching the patient's nostril.

cards.question: What is the advantage of two-rescuer bag-valve-mask ventilation?
cards.answer: One rescuer maintains a two-handed mask seal while the other ventilates, producing a far better seal and more effective ventilation than one rescuer.

cards.question: What does a pulse oximeter measure and what is a concerning trauma reading?
cards.answer: It measures oxygen saturation (SpO2); a reading below 95% in a trauma patient warrants supplemental oxygen and reassessment.

cards.question: How does a supraglottic (BIAD) airway work?
cards.answer: It is inserted blindly into the hypopharynx and seals over the glottic opening to allow ventilation without visualizing the cords.

cards.question: When are supraglottic airways especially useful?
cards.answer: When intubation is difficult or unavailable, as a rapid primary or rescue airway requiring minimal neck movement.

cards.question: What does the mnemonic SOAP-ME stand for in airway preparation?
cards.answer: Suction, Oxygen, Airways (adjuncts/tubes), Pharmacology, Monitoring, and Equipment (laryngoscope/backup).

cards.question: What cervical-spine precaution is maintained during trauma intubation?
cards.answer: Manual in-line stabilization of the cervical spine throughout the procedure.

cards.question: How is endotracheal tube placement confirmed?
cards.answer: Primarily by waveform capnography, plus visible chest rise and bilateral breath sounds; reconfirm after every move.

cards.question: When is nasotracheal intubation contraindicated?
cards.answer: When a basilar skull fracture is suspected and in apneic patients (it requires spontaneous breathing).

cards.question: Why must the tube be anchored and depth noted?
cards.answer: To prevent displacement during movement; note centimeter depth at the teeth so dislodgement can be detected and corrected.

cards.question: What must always be available before giving a paralytic for intubation?
cards.answer: The ability to ventilate (BVM), monitoring (SpO2/capnography), suction, and a rescue airway/backup plan.

cards.question: What is external laryngeal manipulation used for during laryngoscopy?
cards.answer: Applying pressure on the larynx (BURP) to bring the vocal cords into view and improve first-pass intubation success.

cards.question: What is a common cause of failed BVM ventilation?
cards.answer: A poor mask seal; correct with a two-handed/two-rescuer technique and proper airway positioning with an adjunct.

cards.question: Why preoxygenate before intubation?
cards.answer: To build an oxygen reserve that prolongs safe apnea time and prevents desaturation during the attempt.

cards.question: What is the purpose of apneic oxygenation during intubation?
cards.answer: Delivering oxygen (e.g., nasal cannula) during the attempt to extend the time before the patient desaturates.

cards.question: What should the team do if an intubation attempt fails and the patient desaturates?
cards.answer: Stop, ventilate with BVM and reoxygenate, then either reattempt with optimization or place a supraglottic rescue airway.
